prompt:
Use case: stylized-concept
Asset type: project-bound RPG steampunk fantasy atlas map, first batch pass, landscape orientation
Primary request: Iron Kingdoms / Full Metal Fantasy inspired steampunk world map, continent plus archipelago terrain type.
Scene/backdrop: overhead birds-eye atlas view of one dominant industrial fantasy continent with a large uneven offshore archipelago.
Subject: one main steampunk continent with complex coastlines, industrial gulfs, bays, capes, narrow straits, broken rocky coasts, river deltas, inland seas, and many irregular island chains offshore. Islands must be unevenly distributed with varied sizes and shapes: fortress islands, tiny dockyard islets, volcanic islands, reef rings, lagoons, narrow channels, remote atolls, warjack foundry islands, alchemist quarantine islands, and storm-lashed naval stations. Include mechanikal factory-cities, steam-vented ruins, warjack assembly ports, arcane storm fronts, rail lines, river cities, blighted marshes, coal mountains, forests, tundra, deserts, wetlands, and coastal dockyards.
Style/medium: Iron Kingdoms continent map, steampunk fantasy aesthetic, mechanikal detail overlay, aged industrial parchment, brass and blueprint cartography, highly detailed illustrated RPG sourcebook map.
Composition/framing: wide 16:9 landscape, dominant continent offset to one side and archipelago sweeping diagonally around it, no simple grid spacing, full map visible.
Lighting/mood: full metal fantasy, steam and magic intertwined, warjack smoke plumes, arcane lightning, dangerous naval routes, mercenary contracts and industrial war.
Color palette: aged brass, soot grey, copper, storm blue, arcane cyan, muted green forests, iron red foundries, parchment tan, coal black.
Materials/textures: stained parchment, brass-etched coastlines, blueprint overlays, riveted border details, hand-painted terrain, fine cartographic ink.
Constraints: no text labels, no letters, no readable words, no legend, no compass text, no watermark, no UI, no characters or portraits. Islands should feel natural and irregular, not evenly spaced.
